title: Postgres MVCC primer
text: Postgres uses Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC). Every transaction sees a snapshot of the database from the moment it began. Writes never block reads; reads never block writes. Each row is stored as a tuple with xmin (creating txid) and xmax (deleting txid) headers. Old tuples are reclaimed by VACUUM, not by transaction commit. This means dead tuples accumulate during long-running transactions and bloat the table until VACUUM runs. Autovacuum handles this in normal operation.

title: Cosine similarity vs dot product
text: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors and is invariant to magnitude. Dot product is sensitive to magnitude. For embeddings normalized to unit length, cosine similarity and dot product are equivalent. Most production embedding models (OpenAI ada-002, sentence-transformers) return normalized vectors, so dot product is faster to compute and gives identical ranking results. Always check whether your model normalizes before deciding.

title: BM25 explained
text: BM25 is a bag-of-words ranking function based on term frequency (TF) and inverse document frequency (IDF). Unlike cosine over embeddings, BM25 is exact-match: 'database' and 'databases' are different terms unless you stem. BM25 has two tunable parameters: k1 (saturation curve for TF) and b (document length normalization). Default values k1=1.2, b=0.75 are reasonable. BM25 outperforms vector search on keyword-heavy queries, named entities, and rare terms.

title: Hybrid retrieval with RRF
text: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines multiple ranked lists into one. For each document d, RRF score = sum over rankers of 1/(k + rank_i(d)) where k is typically 60. RRF is robust to score-scale differences between rankers (BM25 scores and cosine similarities have wildly different ranges). It's the standard way to fuse BM25 + vector search results. Reranking with a cross-encoder on the top-K fused results often beats either ranker alone.

title: What chunking strategy to use
text: Chunking strategies for retrieval: (1) fixed-size character chunks with overlap (simplest, works surprisingly well); (2) sentence-boundary chunks (better for embedding models trained on sentences); (3) semantic chunking by topic shift (expensive, marginal gains); (4) markdown-section-aware chunking (best for technical documentation). Chunk size matters: 256-512 tokens is a common sweet spot for RAG. Longer chunks dilute the embedding signal; shorter chunks miss context.

title: Vector index ANN tradeoffs
text: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indexes trade recall for speed. HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the most common: high recall, fast queries, slow inserts, large memory. IVF (Inverted File Index, used by Faiss and pgvector) clusters vectors first, then searches within clusters: tunable speed-vs-recall via nprobe. For under 1M vectors, an exact brute-force search (numpy or pgvector with no index) is often fast enough and simpler.

title: Reranking with cross-encoders
text: A cross-encoder takes (query, document) as a pair and outputs a relevance score in one forward pass. This is more accurate than the bi-encoder embedding similarity (which encodes query and document independently) but ~100x slower per pair. Standard pattern: retrieve top-100 with bi-encoder cosine, then rerank with cross-encoder, take top-10. Models like ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 are commonly used for reranking.

title: Refusing out-of-corpus questions
text: A RAG system should refuse to answer when the retrieved chunks don't actually support an answer. Heuristics: if max retrieval score < threshold, refuse. If the LLM-generated answer doesn't include any cited document IDs, suspect hallucination. A stronger approach: use a separate 'is this answerable' classifier or ask the LLM 'do these docs support an answer?' as a pre-step. Test for this explicitly: feed questions whose answers are NOT in the corpus.

title: Citation accuracy in RAG
text: Citation accuracy = of the citations the model produced, what fraction are actually relevant to the answer? A common failure mode: the model retrieves doc X, generates text from doc Y (or hallucinates), but cites X. Test by: include a question where the answer fact is in doc A, but the retriever returns docs B and C as top results. The model must say 'I don't know' rather than cite B or C. Strong citation accuracy is what separates RAG from a chatbot with vibes.

title: Conversational RAG and coreference
text: Multi-turn RAG breaks down at coreference. Turn 1: 'What is BM25?' (retrieve docs about BM25). Turn 2: 'Can it handle typos?' — 'it' refers to BM25 from Turn 1, but a naive retriever fed only 'Can it handle typos?' gets nothing relevant. Solutions: (a) rewrite the user's query using LLM with conversation history before retrieval; (b) include conversation history in the embedding; (c) maintain a 'topic' state. Query rewriting is the most common production approach.

title: LangChain at a glance
text: LangChain is a Python/JS framework for orchestrating LLM applications. Core abstractions: PromptTemplates, LLMs, OutputParsers, Chains (sequences), Agents (LLM + tool routing), Retrievers (search backends). LangChain shines for prototyping and for cases needing tool routing or multi-step reasoning. It's heavier than direct API calls — adopt it when you've felt the absence of these abstractions, not by default.

title: LangChain Agents and tool selection
text: A LangChain Agent is an LLM that, given a user query and a list of tools (functions with descriptions), decides which tool(s) to call and in what order. The classic agent pattern is ReAct: Thought → Action → Observation → ... → Answer. Tool selection accuracy is the agent's hardest property to get right. Test it: give the agent a tool list containing tools that are tempting but wrong; check it picks the right one.

title: Connection pooling and async I/O
text: Use a connection pool for the database. asyncpg in Python supports asyncio-native pooling. Default pool size of 10-20 is fine for a single web process. Set max_overflow if your traffic is bursty. Don't share a single Connection across coroutines — share a Pool instead. SQLAlchemy 2.x async supports both patterns. For Postgres on AWS RDS, account for the connection limit (50-100 per t3.micro).

title: Idempotent writes via natural keys
text: An idempotent write produces the same result whether called once or N times. The cheapest path: insert with a natural key (deterministic from inputs) and ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING in Postgres. For SQS/Kafka consumers, use the message id. For HTTP POSTs, accept an Idempotency-Key header (Stripe popularized this). The store needs a (key, response) cache so retries return the cached response without re-running the side effect.

title: Rate limiting algorithms
text: Three common rate limiting algorithms: (1) Fixed window — simple but suffers from burstiness at boundaries; (2) Sliding window log — exact but O(N) memory per key; (3) Token bucket — smooth, allows bursts up to bucket size, regenerates at fixed rate. Token bucket is the production default. Implement in Redis with Lua for atomicity, or in Postgres with a pessimistic update. The hard part isn't the algorithm; it's the key design (per-user vs per-IP vs per-IP-per-route) and how you handle quota exhaustion (429 with Retry-After).
